theater_description: Collapse-window demolition of the main bridge crossing during the June fall of Seoul.
geometry_method_note: Bounded to the bridge and immediate crossing approaches.
military_area_note: Episode captures the demolition event itself rather than the whole June battle for the capital.
civilian_area_note: Mixed bridge users are not separable into civilian and military classes from the open source used here.
split_reason: Kept separate because a single bridge-demolition event is much cleaner than a generic 'fall of Seoul' row.
quality_note: Date and mixed death range are clear; class split and denominator remain weak.

theater_description: Hospital massacre during the June fall of Seoul.
military_area_note: Source suggests some wounded ROK soldiers were among the dead, but the open source is internally inconsistent on exact class totals.
civilian_area_note: Hospital staff, patients, and inpatient civilians dominate the source description.
split_reason: Separated from both the general June fall and the September recapture because the victim pool is institutionally bounded.
quality_note: Strong event identity, but open-source fatality totals are internally inconsistent and should not be over-precisified.

geographic_scope: Han River crossings and central Seoul recapture corridor
theater_description: Urban recapture phase conducted by U.N./ROK forces after Inchon.
geometry_method_note: Bounded to the Seoul recapture corridor discussed in the Marine Corps monograph, not to all X Corps operations elsewhere in Korea.
military_area_note: Military deaths are best documented for U.S. Marine divisional casualties; hostile-force fatality claims are much weaker and may include broader campaign effects.
civilian_area_note: No clean Seoul-only civilian direct-death total is available in the open sources reviewed here.
split_reason: Separated from June subepisodes to avoid blending the city's fall and recapture into one pseudo-episode.
quality_note: Best available Seoul-recapture military numerators exist, but city-only denominators and civilian deaths do not.
